title: cache_poisoning.canary
description: Web cache poisoning proven on a PRIVATE engine-owned cache key: an unkeyed input in the poison request plants an engine canary into a cached response, a later clean request (same key, no canary input) receives the canary, and a control on a different key does not.
x-notes: Bounded, no-third-party-harm design. Each request MUST carry a {{cachebuster}} slot in the URL, body, or headers; the engine fills it with a fresh high-entropy private token, so the poisoned cache entry is keyed to a value no real client will ever request. Per repetition the engine uses a NEW private key for poison+clean and a DIFFERENT private key for the control. The poison_request MUST also carry a {{canary}} slot (in the unkeyed input — e.g. an X-Forwarded-Host header or a reflected, non-keyed parameter) that the engine fills with a 128-bit nonce.

The {{cachebuster}} token and the {{canary}} nonce are distinct and share no substring, so reflecting the cache buster cannot be mistaken for the canary. clean_request and control_request MUST NOT contain {{canary}} — they are victim-shaped fetches.

verified requires: poison succeeds; the clean fetch (same key, no canary input) returns the canary in its body or headers; the control fetch (different key) does not; stable across repetitions. This proves an unkeyed input is cached and served to a different request sharing the key — it does NOT assert a specific shared/global key is poisonable against real users. Promote that to a PoC-level claim only with operator-authorized evidence.

side_effects.cleanup literal requests run on every exit path. Because the keys are private and high-entropy, the only client that fetches them is the engine; entries otherwise expire by the cache TTL.
